result:
The light through my window this morning was the particular gold of late September—thin, slanted, and full of goodbye. It caught the dust motes in the air, turning them into a swirl of tiny, frantic constellations. I watched them, coffee cooling in my hand, and thought about how we are all just temporary arrangements of borrowed atoms, dancing in a fleeting sunbeam.

My mind drifted to a conversation I had 15 years ago with an old man in a park. He was feeding pigeons with a bag of stale bread, and I, too anxious to sit still, was pacing. He didn't look up, just said, “You’re wearing a path in the grass. The earth notices, you know.” I stopped, irritated and intrigued. We talked. He told me he’d been a carpenter, then a teacher, then a traveler after his wife died. “I’ve been assembling and disassembling my life the whole time,” he said. “Just like these pigeons. They gather, they scatter. The pattern is the only constant.”

I think of him often. I never knew his name. But his idea—that we are in a state of perpetual assembly and disassembly—has become a quiet comfort. We build a career, a home, an identity. We cling to it, call it solid. Then a wind comes—a loss, a change, a simple revelation—and the arrangement scatters. We are left with the pieces, and the choice to build something new, or to sit for a while amongst the scattered parts.

We are so afraid of the scattering. We see it as failure, as chaos. But what if the scattering is just the universe’s way of asking, “Are you sure that shape still fits you?” The leaves outside are scattering in brilliant reds and yellows. They are not dying in disgrace; they are performing a final, glorious dance before returning to the soil to become something else. Next spring’s green will be built, in part, from this gold.

So this morning, in this particular light, I feel a strange peace with my own scatterings. The ambitions that dissolved, the relationships that transformed, the certainties that evaporated. They are not proof of a life poorly built, but of a life that is alive—responsive, breathing, adapting. I am not the same person I was when I paced in that park. I am a new arrangement, built from the old parts and some new ones, sitting in a new sunbeam.

The coffee is gone. The dust motes have settled. The light has shifted, whitened, become the light of ordinary day. The magic hour is over. But the thought remains: maybe the goal isn’t to build a monument that will never fall, but to learn the gentle, brave art of reassembly. To greet each new, temporary shape of ourselves with a little curiosity, a little grace, and to thank the old shapes as they rejoin the scatter.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
